title: Grass on Polyester
stainChemistry: Grass stains contain chlorophyll (green pigment), proteins, and cellulose fibers. The green color comes from chlorophyll's porphyrin ring, which bonds to polyester through a combination of protein adhesion and pigment staining. The protein component sets with heat. The chlorophyll pigment is soluble in alcohol and responds to oxidizers.
whyThisWorks: Treatment addresses both components: enzyme or surfactant breaks down the protein matrix on polyester, then an oxidizer or alcohol-based solvent targets the chlorophyll pigment. Cold water prevents the protein from setting. The pigment requires a secondary treatment after the protein is addressed.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein

homeSolutions.agent: Two parts to fight: protein + chlorophyll
homeSolutions.instruction: Grass stain is the green pigment (chlorophyll) PLUS the plant protein it's bound to. Each needs different chemistry: Dawn + cold water handles the protein, rubbing alcohol handles the chlorophyll. Doing only one half is why half-treated grass stains come back green after the first wash.

Polyester is heat-sensitive — cold water only (hot water shrinks/melts polyester AND sets plant protein). No tumble-dry, no iron until stain is fully gone — heat fuses chlorophyll into polyester at low dryer temps. On workout polyester specifically, skip fabric softener — it coats the fiber and blocks your enzyme step.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden seam
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a drop of isopropyl alcohol 91% on an inside seam and blot with a white cloth. If no dye transfers, you can use alcohol safely in step 5. If the seam loses color, skip alcohol and go step 4 → 6 only (alcohol is the heavy lifter for grass — without it, expect partial removal).

Apply a small drop of Dawn directly on the stain. Work it in with a fingertip for 30 seconds. Let sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn emulsifies the plant fats and loosens the protein matrix so the alcohol in step 5 can reach the chlorophyll underneath.

This is THE step for grass stains. Dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on a clean white cloth or Q-tip and press onto the green stain. Work from outside edges in. Replace the cloth as it picks up green — chlorophyll is alcohol-soluble, so you'll literally see the green transfer to the cloth. Keep going until no more green comes up. 2–3 minutes of patient dabbing does more than an hour of scrubbing with anything else.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use alcohol on silk, rayon, wool, or acetate. Polyester and cotton tolerate it fine. Keep away from open flame — alcohol is flammable. Ventilate the room.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply an enzyme stain remover (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) per label and let dwell 20–30 minutes. Then launder on cold with your normal detergent. Enzymes digest any remaining plant protein. Skip fabric softener this wash — it can trap residual chlorophyll.

Hang or lay flat to air-dry. Inspect in daylight before going anywhere near the dryer. If any green trace remains — even faint — repeat steps 5–6. Only put it in the dryer once the stain is invisible in natural light. Heat on residual chlorophyll sets it permanently on polyester.

If the stain was already through a hot dryer, sat for more than a week, or survived two home rounds, take it in. Tell them: "Grass on polyester, age [fresh/days/heat-set], treated with Dawn + isopropyl alcohol + enzyme. No hot water used at home." Pros use commercial chlorophyll solvents + protein digestants that outperform home alcohol.

steps: 1. Rinse immediately with COLD water. Blot with a clean white cloth — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup cold water. Apply to stain and blot
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat soap application until stain fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all soap residue
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer or any heat until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is grass on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
